claim: attention mechanisms allow neural machine translation models to align source and target words
sequence transduction can be done entirely with self-attention, without recurrence or convolution
a generator and a discriminator trained in a minimax game can learn to produce realistic samples
identity shortcut connections let very deep networks be trained by learning residual functions

normalizing layer inputs over each mini-batch reduces internal covariate shift and speeds up training
adaptive estimates of first and second moments of gradients give an effective stochastic optimizer
word vectors trained by predicting context words capture syntactic and semantic regularities
bidirectional pre-training with masked language modeling produces strong transferable language representations
prompting language models to produce intermediate reasoning steps improves performance on math word problems
generative models can synthesize images by learning to reverse a gradual noising process

an encoder-decoder pair of recurrent networks can map input sequences to output sequences of different lengths
gating mechanisms with a constant error carousel solve the vanishing gradient problem in recurrent networks
very large language models can perform new tasks from a few in-context examples without parameter updates
fine-tuning language models with reinforcement learning from human feedback aligns them with user instructions

freezing pretrained weights and learning low-rank update matrices makes fine-tuning large models parameter-efficient

normalizing across the features of each sample stabilizes training of recurrent networks

language model loss follows a power law in model size, dataset size, and training compute

combining Monte Carlo tree search with deep value and policy networks can master the game of Go
